frameworks.name: Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: Prior framing of ASD perceptual anomalies the paper situates itself against.

frameworks.name: information-theoretic sensory encoding
frameworks.one_line: Encoding capacity and its allocation are extracted directly from psychophysics.

frameworks.name: efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: Natural-scene orientation statistics shape baseline sensory representations.

core_question: Does sensory encoding capacity and its adaptive re-allocation to changing stimulus statistics differ in Autism Spectrum Disorder?
key_assumptions: Encoding accuracy is recoverable from psychophysical data via information-theoretic measures.
Decoding stage can be bypassed when isolating sensory encoding.
Baseline sensory representations track natural-scene orientation statistics.
manipulations: Stimulus distribution (natural-scene statistics vs artificial experimental statistics).
key_findings: Baseline encoding capacity for visual orientation was lower in the ASD group.
Controls re-allocated encoding toward the artificial distribution and increased total encoding resources after exposure.
ASD group showed no significant change in total encoding resources or their allocation.
Across groups, adaptive re-allocation correlated with initial encoding capacity.
interpretations: Neural encoding resources are limited in ASD.
ASD perceptual anomalies reflect encoding-side irregularities, not only Bayesian decoding differences.
